event: Austronesian-speaking voyagers from Island Southeast Asia settle Guam, making it among the earliest inhabited Pacific islands and the homeland of the ancestral Chamorro people.
Early Chamorro communities establish pottery-making villages along Guam's coasts during what archaeologists call the Pre-Latte Period.
The Latte Period begins as Chamorros raise distinctive latte stones—paired pillars (haligi) topped with capstones (tasa)—to support large communal houses.
Ferdinand Magellan's expedition lands at Guam, the first recorded European contact with the Mariana Islands, and clashes with Chamorros over a stolen skiff.
Spanish navigator Miguel Lopez de Legazpi formally claims Guam and the Marianas for the Spanish crown.
Jesuit priest Diego Luis de San Vitores founds the first Catholic mission on Guam, beginning systematic Spanish colonization and conversion of the Chamorros.
Father San Vitores is killed by Chamorro chief Mata'pang, sparking decades of Chamorro resistance to Spanish rule.
Spain appoints its first resident governor of the Marianas, with Guam as the administrative center, intensifying military reduction of Chamorro villages.
The Spanish-Chamorro Wars and introduced epidemics end with the Chamorro population devastated, reduced to a fraction of its pre-contact numbers.
Spanish authorities expel the Jesuits from Guam, transferring the missions to other religious orders.
French explorer Louis de Freycinet visits Guam, producing detailed scientific and ethnographic accounts of the island under Spanish rule.
A devastating smallpox epidemic sweeps Guam, killing a large share of the surviving Chamorro population.
The USS Charleston captures Guam from Spain during the Spanish-American War, and the Treaty of Paris cedes the island to the United States.
Spain sells the rest of the Northern Marianas to Germany, splitting the Mariana Islands as Guam becomes a U.S. naval station.
The U.S. Navy establishes military governance over Guam, ruling the island by gubernatorial decree for the next half-century.
The U.S. Navy seizes the German freighter Cormoran in Apra Harbor when its crew scuttles it as the United States enters World War I.
Japanese forces invade and capture Guam days after Pearl Harbor, beginning a harsh wartime occupation of the Chamorro population.
U.S. forces retake Guam in the Battle of Guam, liberating the island after roughly two and a half years of Japanese occupation.
Guam's people petition Washington for civil rights and self-government, pressing for an end to naval rule and recognition of U.S. citizenship.
The U.S. Congress passes the Organic Act of Guam, granting U.S. citizenship to Chamorros and establishing civilian territorial government.
President Kennedy lifts the Navy's longstanding security clearance requirement, opening Guam to free travel and spurring economic growth.
Congress authorizes Guamanians to elect their own governor, replacing the federally appointed governor.
Carlos Camacho wins the first popular election for governor of Guam.
Guam gains a non-voting delegate seat in the U.S. House of Representatives, electing Antonio Borja Won Pat as its first delegate.
Japanese soldier Shoichi Yokoi, who had hidden in Guam's jungles since World War II, is discovered nearly 28 years after the war ended.
Typhoon Pamela strikes Guam with catastrophic force, destroying much of the island's infrastructure and prompting major rebuilding.
In a plebiscite, Guam voters favor commonwealth status, launching a long campaign to redefine the island's political relationship with the United States.
Korean Air Flight 801 crashes on approach to Guam, killing more than 200 people in one of the island's worst aviation disasters.
Super Typhoon Pongsona devastates Guam, causing widespread destruction and prompting a federal disaster declaration.
The United States and Japan agree to relocate thousands of U.S. Marines from Okinawa to Guam, beginning a major military buildup plan.
North Korea threatens to launch missiles toward Guam, drawing global attention to the island's strategic role hosting major U.S. military bases.
A federal appeals court strikes down Guam's planned self-determination plebiscite as unconstitutional for limiting voting to Native Inhabitants.
The aircraft carrier USS Theodore Roosevelt diverts to Guam amid a major COVID-19 outbreak among its crew, spotlighting the island during the pandemic.
Typhoon Mawar, the strongest storm to hit Guam in decades, batters the island and knocks out power and water for much of the population.
U.S. Marines formally activate Camp Blaz, the first new Marine Corps base in 70 years, as personnel relocate from Okinawa to Guam.
